text_blocks.snippet: Dark matter is a mysterious, invisible substance that makes up about 85% of all matter in the universe. It doesn't emit, absorb, or reflect light, and can only be detected through its gravitational pull on visible matter like stars and galaxies.
snippet_highlighted_words: a mysterious, invisible substance that makes up about 85% of all matter in the universe

text_blocks.snippet: Astronomers can trace dark matter by its invisible weight and the way it acts as a cosmic glue. Key pieces of evidence include:

list.snippet: Galaxy Rotation: In the 1970s, astronomer Vera Rubin discovered that stars at the outer edges of galaxies were orbiting just as fast as those near the center. Based on the visible mass, gravity should have been too weak to hold them in, implying a massive halo of unseen matter.

text_blocks.snippet: Scientists know dark matter isn't made of normal "baryonic" matter (like protons and neutrons). The leading theories suggest it consists of undiscovered subatomic particles. The most popular candidates are Weakly Interacting Massive Particles (WIMPs)—hypothetical particles that have mass but rarely interact with normal matter.

If you are interested in the ongoing search for these particles or how scientists map the "cosmic web," you can learn more about current experiments through the Department of Energy or the European Space Agency.

extracted.summary:
![Dark Matter - NASA Science](https://api.dataforseo.com/cdn/i/07210107-2120-0139-0000-a5438dc4194a:4)
Dark matter is `a mysterious, invisible substance that makes up about 85% of all matter in the universe` . It doesn't emit, absorb, or reflect light, and can only be detected through its gravitational pull on visible matter like stars and galaxies.[](https://www.energy.gov/science/doe-explainsdark-matter) [[1]](https://www.energy.gov/science/doe-explainsdark-matter)[[2]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tvAR36l2o1g&t=4)[[3]](https://www.lsst.org/science/dark-matter)

Why We Know It Exists

Astronomers can trace dark matter by its invisible weight and the way it acts as a cosmic glue. Key pieces of evidence include:[](https://spaceplace.nasa.gov/dark-matter/) [[1]](https://spaceplace.nasa.gov/dark-matter/)

- **Galaxy Rotation:** In the 1970s, astronomer Vera Rubin discovered that stars at the outer edges of galaxies were orbiting just as fast as those near the center. Based on the visible mass, gravity should have been too weak to hold them in, implying a massive halo of unseen matter.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tvAR36l2o1g&t=4) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tvAR36l2o1g&t=4)[[2]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yRC_zISpYNQ&t=13)
- **Gravitational Lensing:** Because dark matter has mass, it warps spacetime. When light from distant, background galaxies travels past heavy clusters of dark matter, the light bends and distorts—acting like a magnifying glass.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tvAR36l2o1g&t=4) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tvAR36l2o1g&t=4)[[2]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KurVp4-aSQ0&t=14)

What Could It Be?Scientists know dark matter isn't made of normal "baryonic" matter (like protons and neutrons). The leading theories suggest it consists of undiscovered subatomic particles. The most popular candidates are Weakly Interacting Massive Particles (WIMPs)—hypothetical particles that have mass but rarely interact with normal matter.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yRC_zISpYNQ&t=13) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yRC_zISpYNQ&t=13)[[2]](https://www.britannica.com/science/dark-matter)[[3]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/4mKvBHjBsw8)[[4]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KurVp4-aSQ0&t=14)If you are interested in the ongoing search for these particles or how scientists map the "cosmic web," you can learn more about current experiments through the [Department of Energy](https://www.energy.gov/science/doe-explainsdark-matter) or the [European Space Agency](https://sci.esa.int/web/euclid/-/what-is-dark-matter-).[[1]](https://www.lsst.org/science/dark-matter)
